analogy: Think of "Nationalism" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 7 Nationalism Overview This chapter will introduce and discuss the ideas of nationalism and nation. Our concern will be not so much to understand why nationalism has arisen, or what functions it serves; rather our concern would be to think carefully about nationalism and... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 7.1 INTRODUCING NATIONALISM, 7.2 NATIONS AND NATIONALISM, LET’S DO IT D o, 7.3 NATIONAL SELF-DETERMINATION. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Nationalism is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 7 Nationalism Overview This chapter will introduce and discuss the ideas of nationalism and nation.

The text explains that our concern will be not so much to understand why nationalism has arisen, or what functions it serves; rather our concern would be to think carefully about nationalism and assess its claims and aspirations.

The text explains that after studying this chapter you should be able to: o understand the concepts of nation and nationalism. o acknowledge the strengths and limitations of nationalism. o appreciate the need for ensuring a link between democracy and nationalism.

The text explains that nationalismNationalismPolitical Theory 98 7.1 INTRODUCING NATIONALISM If we were to take a quick poll of what people commonly understand by the term nationalism we are likely to get responses which talk about patriotism, national flags, sacrificing for the country, and the like.

The text explains that the Republic Day parade in Delhi is a striking symbol of Indian nationalism and it brings out the sense of power, strength, as well as diversity which many associate with the Indian nation.

The text explains that but if we try to go deeper we will find that it is difficult to arrive at a precise and widely accepted definition of the term nationalism.

The text explains that this need not mean that we should abandon the effort.

The text explains that nationalism needs to be studied because it plays such an important role in world affairs.

The text explains that during the last two centuries or more, nationalism has emerged as one of the most compelling of political creeds which has helped to shape history.

The text explains that it has inspired intense loyalties as well as deep hatreds.

The text explains that it has united people as well as divided them, helped to liberate them from oppressive rule as well as been the cause of conflict and bitterness and wars.

The text explains that it has been a factor in the break up of empires and states.

The text explains that nationalist struggles have contributed to the drawing and redrawing of the boundaries of states and empires.

The text explains that at present a large part of the world is divided into different nation-states although the process of re-ordering of state boundaries has not come to an end and separatist struggles within existing states are common.

The text explains that for instance, in the nineteenth century Europe, it led to the unification of a number of small kingdoms into larger nation-states.

The text explains that the present day German and Italian states were formed through such a process of unification and consolidation.

The text explains that we have seen a similar process of consolidation taking place in our own country in the last century or more.

The text explains that the process of redrawing state boundaries continues to take place.

The text explains that do nations have a right to statehood or national self-determination?

The text explains that in this chapter we will explore some of these issues.

The text explains that at the same time it is also different from other groups or communities found in human society.

The text explains that it is different from the family which is based on face-to-face relationships with each member having direct personal knowledge of the identity and character of others.

The text explains that it is commonly believed that nations are constituted by a group who share certain features such as descent, or language, or religion or ethnicity.

The text explains that history Second, people who see themselves as a nation also embody a sense of continuing historical identity.

The text explains that they articulate for themselves a sense of their own history by drawing on collective memories, legends, historical records, to outline the continuing identity of the nation.

The text explains that thus nationalists in India invoked its ancient civilisation and cultural heritage and other achievements to claim that India has had a long and continuing history as a civilisation and that this civilisational continuity and unity is the basis of the Indian nation.

The text explains that nationalismNationalismPolitical Theory 103 arise from the recognition of the rights of each other as citizens.

The text explains that common Political Identity Many people believe that a shared political vision about the state and society we wish to create is not enough to bind individuals together as a nation.

The text explains that if we ignore these differences and forge an identity on the basis of a common religion we are likely to create a highly authoritative and oppressive society.

The text explains that for both these reasons it is desirable to imagine the nation in political rather than cultural terms.

The text explains that that is, democracies need to emphasise and expect loyalty to a set of values that may be enshrined in the Constitution of the country rather than adherence to a particular religion, race or language.

The text explains that but we are still left with an important question, namely, why do people imagine themselves as a nation?

The text explains that in the next two sections we shall try to address these questions.

The text explains that 7.3 NATIONAL SELF-DETERMINATION Nations, unlike other social groups, seek the right to govern themselves and determine their future development.

The text explains that they seek, in other words, the right to self-determination.

The text explains that this region is recognised by the Spanish government as an ‘autonomous’ region within the Spanish federation.

The text explains that supporters of this movement have used constitutional and, till recently, violent means to press for this demand.

The text explains that its systems of justice, administration and finance were governed by its own unique arrangements.

The text explains that but the leaders of Basque movement continue to be suspicious of the motives of the Spanish government and fearful of the entry of ‘outsiders’ in their region.

The text explains that what more would you like to know before you can answer this question?

The text explains that source: Multiple Resources including www.en.wikipedia.org

The text explains that the right to national self-determination has also been asserted by national liberation movements in Asia and Africa when they were struggling against colonial domination.

The text explains that most national liberation movements were inspired by the goal of bringing justice and rights and prosperity to the nation.

The text explains that thus we have the paradoxical situation of nation-states which themselves had achieved independence through struggle now acting against minorities within their own territories who claim the right to national self- determination.

The text explains that virtually every state in the world today faces the dilemma of how to deal with movements for self-determination and this has raised questions about the right to national self-determination.

The text explains that after all, a nation-state which does not respect the rights

The text explains that the Indian constitution has an elaborate set of provisions for the protection of religious, linguistic and cultural minorities.

The text explains that it would be dangerous if intolerant and homogenising forms of identity and nationalism are allowed to develop.

The text explains that nationalismNationalismPolitical Theory 110 1.

The text explains that how is a nation different from other forms of collective belonging?

The text explains that what do you understand by the right to national self-determination?

The text explains that how has this idea resulted in both formation of and challenges to nation-states?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 7: Nationalism
